statement: The nebular hypothesis — explaining how solar systems form from gas and dust — was first published by Immanuel Kant in 1755, then independently refined by Pierre Laplace in 1796.

wowFactor: A philosopher — not an astronomer — gave us our best model for how planets form. Kant's 1755 book on nebulae predated the discovery of most planets in our solar system!

statement: The Hertzsprung-Russell diagram plots stars by luminosity vs. temperature, revealing the main sequence and stellar evolution stages.

wowFactor: The HR diagram reveals that stars don't exist in random combinations — gravity and nuclear physics force them into precise bands, and a star's position on the diagram predicts its entire life story.

statement: A black hole's event horizon is the boundary beyond which nothing, not even light, can escape its gravity.

wowFactor: From outside the event horizon, time appears to slow to a complete stop for an infalling observer — but from their perspective, they cross the event horizon without noticing anything special, just before being torn apart.

statement: SpaceX Crew Dragon became the first commercial spacecraft to carry NASA astronauts to the ISS in May 2020 on Demo-2.
quizQuestion: Which commercial spacecraft first carried NASA astronauts to the ISS in May 2020?
explanation: SpaceX Crew Dragon Demo-2 launched May 30, 2020 with astronauts Doug Hurley and Bob Behnken — the first crewed orbital launch from American soil since Space Shuttle Atlantis in 2011. It ended the US dependence on Russian Soyuz vehicles for ISS crew transport after a 9-year gap.
wowFactor: Demo-2 ended 9 years of US dependence on Russian Soyuz for astronaut transport — and it was launched during the COVID-19 pandemic, making those two astronauts also the first people to launch to space mid-global-pandemic.

statement: Super-Earths are exoplanets with masses between Earth and Neptune, making them the most common planet type found in the Milky Way.
quizQuestion: What type of exoplanet has a mass between Earth and Neptune and is the most common found in the galaxy?
explanation: Super-Earths have masses of 1-10 Earth masses. Despite being the most common exoplanet type discovered, our solar system has none. They may be rocky, oceanic, or gaseous depending on composition, and many orbit in habitable zones of red dwarf stars. Kepler data suggests over 40% of Sun-like stars have super-Earths.
wowFactor: Super-Earths — the most common planet type in the galaxy — don't exist anywhere in our solar system. We're the cosmic oddity: a solar system without the most abundant type of world.

statement: The Planck epoch is the earliest period of the universe, from time zero to 10^-43 seconds, where current physics breaks down.
quizQuestion: What is the name of the earliest phase of the universe where current physics cannot describe what happened?
explanation: At time scales below the Planck time (5.4 × 10^-44 seconds), quantum gravitational effects dominate and general relativity breaks down. Without a theory of quantum gravity, physicists cannot describe this epoch. It represents the ultimate limit of our current understanding of the universe.
wowFactor: The Planck epoch lasted 10^-43 seconds — a time so brief there is no conceivable analog. If one second were the age of the universe, the Planck epoch would be shorter than a Planck time is to one second — recursively incomprehensible.

variants.question: Why can't physicists currently describe what happened during the Planck epoch?
variants.correctAnswer: No working theory of quantum gravity exists
distractors: No observations can reach that far back

statement: In 1998, two teams of astronomers discovered the universe's expansion is accelerating, driven by a mysterious force called dark energy.
quizQuestion: What was discovered about the universe's expansion rate in 1998 that earned the Nobel Prize in 2011?
explanation: Using Type Ia supernovae as standard candles, teams led by Saul Perlmutter, Brian Schmidt, and Adam Riess found distant supernovae dimmer than expected — meaning they're farther away than a decelerating universe would predict. The universe's expansion is speeding up, powered by dark energy. They won the 2011 Nobel Prize.
wowFactor: Astronomers expected gravity to be slowly braking the universe's expansion — instead they found it's accelerating like a car pressing harder on the gas, implying an unknown force that outmuscles all the gravity in the universe.

statement: Proxima Centauri is the nearest star to Earth at 4.24 light-years, a red dwarf in the Alpha Centauri triple star system.
quizQuestion: What is the name of the star nearest to Earth, located just 4.24 light-years away?
explanation: Proxima Centauri is a red dwarf (class M5.5) in the Alpha Centauri system, gravitationally bound to the binary pair Alpha Centauri A and B. It's too dim to see with the naked eye despite being our nearest neighbor. It hosts at least one planet (Proxima b) and possibly two others.
wowFactor: Even the nearest star is so far that the fastest spacecraft ever launched — Voyager 1 — would take 73,000 years to reach it at its current speed. Yet we can study its planets already.

statement: NASA's Spitzer Space Telescope operated in infrared light from 2003 to 2020, pioneering infrared astronomy before JWST.
quizQuestion: In what year was NASA's Spitzer Space Telescope decommissioned after 16 years of infrared observation?
explanation: Spitzer launched August 25, 2003 and was decommissioned January 30, 2020 after 16 years — far outlasting its original 2.5-year mission. It mapped galactic structure, studied exoplanet atmospheres (first detected an exoplanet's thermal emission), imaged stellar nurseries, and discovered the TRAPPIST-1 system's full complement of planets.
wowFactor: Spitzer discovered that the TRAPPIST-1 system has seven planets — despite being a ground-breaking discovery in 2017, it was made by a telescope designed in the 1990s that was already running on fumes with depleted coolant.

statement: Christina Koch set the record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman with 328 days aboard the ISS from 2019 to 2020.
quizQuestion: Which astronaut set the record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman with 328 days on the ISS?
explanation: Christina Koch served on Expedition 59, 60, and 61, landing March 6, 2020 after 328 days, 13 hours, 58 minutes — the longest single spaceflight by a woman. She also participated in the first all-woman spacewalk with Jessica Meir in October 2019. Koch later flew on Artemis II.
wowFactor: Koch's 328-day mission was designed specifically to study how long-duration spaceflight affects the female body — her data is helping NASA plan Moon and Mars missions for diverse crews.

statement: NASA's Artemis program aims to return humans to the Moon and land the first woman and first person of color on the lunar surface.
quizQuestion: What is the name of NASA's program to return humans to the Moon, including the first woman on the lunar surface?
explanation: Named for Apollo's twin sister in Greek mythology, Artemis aims to establish a sustainable human presence at the Moon using the SLS rocket and Orion capsule. Artemis I (2022) was an uncrewed test flight. Artemis II (2024 target) will carry four astronauts around the Moon. Artemis III will land on the lunar south pole.
wowFactor: Artemis III aims to land at the lunar south pole — a region permanently shadowed for billions of years that may contain ancient frozen water ice that could support permanent human settlements.
